card.title: Why Apple's Logo Has a Bite
card.teaser: Is it a tribute to Alan Turing? A nod to Newton? Or just a trick to stop it looking like a cherry?
card.coverImagePrompt: A sleek silver apple resting on a dark reflective surface, a single crisp bite taken from its right side, dramatic side lighting, minimalist product photography, cinematic

lessons.title: The internet's favorite urban legend
lessons.body: For decades, one story has refused to die: the bite in the Apple logo is a tribute to Alan Turing, the gay computing pioneer who died in 1954 after eating a cyanide-laced apple. It's a beautiful, tragic story. There's just one problem — it isn't true. The real reason is far more pragmatic, and the designer himself has told us exactly what it was.

visual.imagePrompt: A silhouetted apple with a bite, backlit in rainbow colors fading to grayscale, soft mystery atmosphere

lessons.title: Meet the designer
lessons.body: In 1977, Steve Jobs hired a young art director named Rob Janoff to design a logo for the new Apple II. Janoff sketched a clean silhouette of an apple — but something was off. Without the bite, the silhouette looked suspiciously like a cherry, or worse, a tomato. The logo needed to read as an apple instantly, even at postage-stamp size.

keyTerms.definition: A solid shape representing an object's outline, recognizable at a glance
Whether a design still reads clearly when shrunk very small

visual.imagePrompt: A 1970s designer's desk with pencil sketches of fruit silhouettes on tracing paper, warm tungsten lighting

lessons.title: Bite, not byte
lessons.body: Janoff added the bite so your eye would instantly know: this is an apple, and it's for eating. The pun on 'byte' was a happy accident — Janoff has said he didn't consciously intend it. The original 1977 logo was also rainbow-striped, which had nothing to do with gay pride. The colors were chosen to show off the Apple II's then-revolutionary ability to display color on a home screen.

visual.imagePrompt: The original rainbow-striped Apple logo on the side of a beige 1977 Apple II computer, nostalgic retro tech setting

lessons.title: Straight from the designer
lessons.body: Rob Janoff has been asked about the Turing theory countless times. In interviews he's been gracious but clear: 'I'm afraid it didn't have a thing to do with it.' Stephen Fry once asked Steve Jobs directly. Jobs replied that while the Turing story was a wonderful idea, 'God, we wish it were.' The truth is simpler — and more design-school than conspiracy.
lessons.funFact: The rainbow logo was retired in 1998 when the translucent iMac launched — partly because rainbow stripes didn't survive the transition to flat, minimalist product shells.

visual.imagePrompt: A designer being interviewed on a talk show set, friendly studio lighting, apple logo poster behind him

lessons.title: Why the myth sticks anyway
lessons.body: Good logos invite stories. The bitten apple is simple enough to remember, weird enough to ask about, and meaningful enough that people reach for deeper explanations. That's not a bug — it's the whole point of iconic branding. The real lesson? Great design solves a boring problem (is this a cherry?) in a way that lasts fifty years.

quiz.question: According to designer Rob Janoff, why does the Apple logo have a bite?
options: So it wouldn't look like a cherry
To reference the Garden of Eden
quiz.explanation: Janoff added the bite purely so the silhouette would read as an apple rather than a cherry at small sizes.

quiz.question: Why was the original 1977 Apple logo striped with rainbow colors?
options: To show off the Apple II's color display
To match Pink Floyd's album cover
To reference the rainbow flag of peace
quiz.explanation: The Apple II was one of the first home computers with color graphics, and the logo showcased that capability.

quiz.question: When did Apple retire the rainbow logo?
quiz.explanation: The rainbow logo was phased out in 1998 alongside the launch of the translucent iMac G3.

sources.title: Interview with Rob Janoff, designer of the Apple logo
Stephen Fry on Steve Jobs and the Turing rumor
